word_list.pronunciation: bˈɛɹi hæv wˈʌnz hˈɛd ɪnðə sˈænd

senses.definition: to intentionally ignore unpleasant facts about a situation and hope that the situation improves by doing so

examples.x: Ignoring financial problems won't make them disappear; you can't bury your head in the sand forever.
She had her head in the sand when it came to her health, refusing to see a doctor even though she felt unwell.

senses.definition: in a state in which one is not informed about important things

examples.x: I'm completely in the dark about their plans for the weekend.
They kept us in the dark about the changes to the project until the last minute.
She prefers to work quietly and independently, often leaving her colleagues in the dark about her progress.
Without access to reliable news sources, they were left in the dark about recent events in the world.
The sudden power outage left everyone in the dark, groping for flashlights.

senses.definition: not being aware of recent information about something

examples.x: Since I've been on vacation, I feel completely out of the loop at work.
She's out of the loop when it comes to the latest office gossip.
I haven't checked my email for a week, so I'm completely out of the loop on any updates.
They didn't include him in the meeting, so he's out of the loop regarding the project's progress.
Being out of the loop can make it challenging to stay connected in a fast-paced industry.

senses.definition: to pretend as if one cannot hear someone complaining or asking one something

examples.x: Despite their pleas for help, the authorities turned a deaf ear to the villagers' concerns about the dam.
She tried to talk to him about their relationship problems, but he just turned a deaf ear.
The teacher turned a deaf ear to the student's excuses for not completing the assignment.
Even though they provided evidence of wrongdoing, the company's management turned a deaf ear to their employees' complaints.
Parents should never turn a deaf ear when their children try to discuss important issues with them.

senses.definition: (of statements, warnings, requests, etc.) to be completely ignored

examples.x: Her warnings about the potential risks of the project fell on deaf ears, and they proceeded without considering her concerns.
I tried to explain the importance of time management to him, but it seemed to fall on deaf ears.
The employee's complaints about workplace safety issues fell on deaf ears until there was a serious accident.
The teacher's attempts to get the students to focus on their studies often fell on deaf ears as they were easily distracted.
Despite the doctor's advice to quit smoking, his patient's habit continued to fall on deaf ears.

word_list.word: [go] in one ear and out the other
word_list.pronunciation: ɡˌoʊ ɪn wˈʌn ˈɪɹ ænd ˈaʊt ðɪ ˈʌðɚ

senses.definition: (of information, advice, etc.) to not be taken seriously and hence immediately forgotten

examples.x: I've told him a hundred times to clean his room, but it just goes in one ear and out the other.
The professor's lecture went in one ear and out the other because I was too tired to concentrate.
The safety instructions at work should be taken seriously; don't let them go in one ear and out the other.
No matter how many times I remind him of our meeting, it always goes in one ear and out the other.

senses.definition: used to describe a situation in which an incompetent or inexperienced person is advising others who have no knowledge or experience at all

examples.x: It's like the blind leading the blind in that department.
Her attempt to teach advanced mathematics to the class was like the blind leading the blind.
The group of amateurs trying to fix the car's engine was a classic case of the blind leading the blind.

word_list.pronunciation: fˈɔːl θɹuː ɔːɹ bɪtwˌiːn ðə kɹˈæks

senses.definition: (of a person) to be completely ignored, usually in a system or process

examples.x: Despite the new educational initiatives, some struggling students still managed to fall through the cracks, receiving insufficient support.
In the crowded healthcare system, it's not uncommon for patients with less visible conditions to fall between the cracks, leading to delayed diagnoses.
The social welfare program was designed to assist vulnerable populations, but unfortunately, some individuals still fall through the cracks due to bureaucratic inefficiencies.
The intricate legal procedures sometimes cause deserving asylum seekers to fall through the cracks, facing deportation without proper consideration of their cases.
Even with the company's strict hiring protocols, talented candidates occasionally fall through the cracks, resulting in missed opportunities for both the applicants and the organization.

word_list.pronunciation: fˈɔːl θɹuː ɔːɹ bɪtwˌiːn ðə kɹˈæks

senses.definition: (of something, particularly issues) to be completely overlooked

examples.x: Despite the thorough project management, a few crucial details managed to fall through the cracks, leading to unexpected challenges during the implementation phase.
The company's attempt to streamline its operations inadvertently caused some key customer feedback to fall between the cracks, resulting in missed opportunities for improvement.
The extensive review process aimed at catching errors in the manuscript, but a few typographical mistakes still fell through the cracks and made it to the final publication.
The comprehensive safety protocols in the laboratory were designed to prevent accidents, but occasionally, minor safety concerns could fall through the cracks if not addressed promptly.
Despite the meticulous planning for the event, logistical issues occasionally fall through the cracks, causing last-minute adjustments and challenges for the organizers.

senses.definition: to pretend that something is not happening or is not the case

examples.x: Instead of addressing the financial problems, the company decided to sweep them under the rug and hope they would go away.
In dysfunctional families, it's common for members to sweep their issues under the rug rather than seeking therapy.
Ignoring a health concern and hoping it disappears is like sweeping it under the rug.
It's important to face challenges head-on rather than trying to sweep them under the rug, as they may resurface later.

senses.definition: to pretend as if one cannot see or notice something

examples.x: The manager chose to turn a blind eye to the employee's habitual tardiness because they were friends.
The teacher should not turn a blind eye to cheating during exams; it sets a bad example for the students.
Government officials turned a blind eye to the illegal activities of the powerful corporation.
Despite the evidence of corruption, the authorities seem to be turning a blind eye to the issue.
It's essential for a fair justice system not to turn a blind eye to any form of discrimination.

senses.definition: a strategy in which a person chooses to ignore or evades the truth or potential problems about a particular situation

examples.x: Their ostrich strategy of ignoring customer complaints only led to a worsening reputation.
The company's ostrich plan for dealing with financial troubles was to avoid discussing them and hope they would disappear.
Rather than addressing the environmental concerns, the government seemed to adopt an ostrich strategy, pretending there was no problem.
The manager's ostrich plan of not addressing employee morale issues resulted in a high turnover rate.

word_list.pronunciation: slˈɪp ɔːɹ fˈɔːl θɹuː ðə nˈɛt

senses.definition: (of a person) to be neglected or go unnoticed, particularly by a social or political system that should have been more responsible

examples.x: Despite the thorough screening process, a few exceptional candidates slipped through the net, and we only discovered their qualifications later.
In the complex immigration system, it's not uncommon for certain cases to fall through the net, resulting in individuals facing uncertain legal status.
The comprehensive healthcare program aims to cover everyone, but some individuals with specific needs may still fall through the net without tailored support.
The meticulous investigation had identified potential suspects, but critical evidence somehow fell through the net, leading to a temporary stall in the case.
Despite the school's efforts to provide support, students with learning differences may occasionally slip through the net if their needs are not promptly recognized and addressed.

word_list.pronunciation: slˈɪp ɔːɹ fˈɔːl θɹuː ðə nˈɛt

senses.definition: (of something particularly an issue) to go unnoticed or to be overlooked within a system, plan, or organization, etc.

examples.x: In the audit process, a critical error in the financial records fell through the net, leading to inaccurate financial reporting.
Despite rigorous security measures, a software vulnerability fell through the net, exposing sensitive data to unauthorized access.
During the project review, a key deadline fell through the net, causing delays in the overall timeline.
Despite efforts to screen for potential side effects, a rare adverse reaction to the medication fell through the net during the clinical trial.
In the immigration system, a loophole allowed certain visa applications to fall through the net, leading to unintended gaps in border control.
